(渋谷スクランブル交差点, *Shibuya sukuranburu kōsaten*), commonly known as **Shibuya Crossing**, is a [scramble crossing](/wiki/Pedestrian_scramble "Pedestrian scramble") in [Shibuya](/wiki/Shibuya_(district) "Shibuya (district)"), [Tokyo](/wiki/Tokyo "Tokyo"), Japan. It is located near [Shibuya Station](/wiki/Shibuya_Station "Shibuya Station") in [Shibuya](/wiki/Shibuya "Shibuya"), a major commercial and entertainment district in Tokyo. It has been described as the world's busiest [pedestrian crossing](/wiki/Pedestrian_crossing "Pedestrian crossing"), with as many as 3,000 people crossing during a single green light cycle.[[1]](#cite_note-asahi_2016-04-22-1)[[2]](#cite_note-cnn_2019-08-25-2)[[3]](#cite_note-3) Inaugurated in 1973, the intersection is a popular tourist destination and a widely recognized symbol of the city of Tokyo through its frequent appearances in television, film, and other media.

["The World's Busiest Pedestrian Crossing"](https://web.archive.org/web/20200812012537/https://www.worldatlas.com/articles/what-is-the-world-s-busiest-pedestrian-crossing.html). *WorldAtlas*. Archived from [the original](https://www.worldatlas.com/articles/what-is-the-world-s-busiest-pedestrian-crossing.html) on August 12, 2020. Retrieved 2020-04-13.

[内山理名](/wiki/%E5%86%85%E5%B1%B1%E7%90%86%E5%90%8D "内山理名"). "内山理名のTokyoフォトアルバム File16 (Japanese book)". *[東京ウォーカー](/w/index.php?title=%E6%9D%B1%E4%BA%AC%E3%82%A6%E3%82%A9%E3%83%BC%E3%82%AB%E3%83%BC&action=edit&redlink=1 "東京ウォーカー (page does not exist)")* (2000年10月17日号). [角川マガジンズ](/w/index.php?title=%E8%A7%92%E5%B7%9D%E3%83%9E%E3%82%AC%E3%82%B8%E3%83%B3%E3%82%BA&action=edit&redlink=1 "角川マガジンズ (page does not exist)"): 85.

伊藤隆太郎 (2016-03-05). "【みちのものがたり】 外国人を引きつける渋谷スクランブル交差点 - 猥雑で混沌とした近未来都市". *[朝日新聞 be](/w/index.php?title=Be_(%E6%9C%9D%E6%97%A5%E6%96%B0%E8%81%9E)&action=edit&redlink=1 "Be (朝日新聞) (page does not exist)")*. p. e2.

| Coordinates: [35°39′34″N 139°42′02″E﻿ / ﻿35.65950°N 139.70056°E﻿ / 35.65950; 139.70056](https://geohack.toolforge.org/geohack.php?pagename=Shibuya_Crossing&params=35.65950_N_139.70056_E_) | |

**Shibuya Scramble Crossing** (渋谷スクランブル交差点, *Shibuya sukuranburu kōsaten*), commonly known as **Shibuya Crossing**, is a [scramble crossing](/wiki/Pedestrian_scramble "Pedestrian scramble") in [Shibuya](/wiki/Shibuya_(district) "Shibuya (district)"), [Tokyo](/wiki/Tokyo "Tokyo"), Japan. It is located near [Shibuya Station](/wiki/Shibuya_Station "Shibuya Station") in [Shibuya](/wiki/Shibuya "Shibuya"), a major commercial and entertainment district in Tokyo. It has been described as the world's busiest [pedestrian crossing](/wiki/Pedestrian_crossing "Pedestrian crossing"), with as many as 3,000 people crossing during a single green light cycle.[[1]](#cite_note-asahi_2016-04-22-1)[[2]](#cite_note-cnn_2019-08-25-2)[[3]](#cite_note-3) Inaugurated in 1973, the intersection is a popular tourist destination and a widely recognized symbol of the city of Tokyo through its frequent appearances in television, film, and other media.

## Usage

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Shibuya_Crossing&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Usage")]

This intersection is frequently recognized as "the busiest pedestrian intersection in the world"[[1]](#cite_note-asahi_2016-04-22-1)[[2]](#cite_note-cnn_2019-08-25-2) with almost no loss of foot traffic at midnight or early morning.[[4]](#cite_note-tokyo_walker_2000-10-17-4) Road traffic jams rarely occur, even during [rush hours](/wiki/Rush_hour "Rush hour").[[2]](#cite_note-cnn_2019-08-25-2) According to the Shibuya Center Street in 2016, the number of pedestrians crossing the intersection was as much as 3,000[[1]](#cite_note-asahi_2016-04-22-1) per green light (every 2 minutes).[[5]](#cite_note-asahi_be_e2-5) A 2014 flow measurement survey by the Shibuya Redevelopment Association estimated that 260,000 pedestrians per day on week days, and 390,000 pedestrians on non-working days crossed the intersection.[[1]](#cite_note-asahi_2016-04-22-1) Others estimate as many as 500,000 people on the busiest days.[[6]](#cite_note-journey-of-japan_2018-08-02-6) The 2012 SOTO Outdoor Media Survey estimated 1.5 million pedestrians per week.[[7]](#cite_note-tokyu_2013-07-11-7)

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Shibuya_Crossing&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: History")]

Pedestrians using the crossing in 2008

The crossing was inaugurated in 1973.[[8]](#cite_note-8) It was featured in the [2016 Summer Olympics closing ceremony](/wiki/2016_Summer_Olympics_closing_ceremony "2016 Summer Olympics closing ceremony") to promote the [2020 Summer Olympics](/wiki/2020_Summer_Olympics "2020 Summer Olympics") in Tokyo.[[9]](#cite_note-9)[[10]](#cite_note-10)

Since the late 2010s it has become a popular place for young people to gather at Halloween, some in [cosplay](/wiki/Cosplay "Cosplay"). Increasingly large and chaotic crowds led to Shibuya Ward adopting an ordinance in 2019 banning public alcohol consumption in the area around the end of October (Halloween) and December (New Year's Eve).[[11]](#cite_note-11)

In 2023, citing issues with litter, altercations, and property damage caused by [overtourism](/wiki/Overtourism "Overtourism") and large, unruly crowds (especially after the lifting of [COVID-19 pandemic](/wiki/COVID-19_pandemic_in_Japan "COVID-19 pandemic in Japan") restrictions, and in the wake of the [Seoul Halloween crowd crush](/wiki/Seoul_Halloween_crowd_crush "Seoul Halloween crowd crush") in 2022), Shibuya Ward officials announced that it would strongly enforce measures to discourage parties on the crossing during the Halloween period, including asking residents and tourists to not gather for Halloween, traffic restrictions, and enhanced security measures to enforce the alcohol ban. Stores in the area were also requested to not sell alcohol, and some businesses closed sooner than usual.[[12]](#cite_note-12)[[13]](#cite_note-13)

Shibuya Crossing has also suspended New Year's Eve celebrations since 2020–21 due to safety and security issues; in 2023, alcohol consumption was restricted from 6 p.m. on 31 December to 5 a.m. on 1 January, and stores were requested to not sell alcohol during this timeframe. In addition, all video billboards were turned off at 11 p.m. rather than 12 a.m. as usual.[[14]](#cite_note-14)[[15]](#cite_note-15) In June 2024, an ordinance was passed to extend the public drinking ban year-round from 6 p.m. to 5 a.m. nightly.[[16]](#cite_note-16)

## In popular culture

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Shibuya_Crossing&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: In popular culture")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e7/Early_summer_night_Shibuya.jpg/250px-Early_summer_night_Shibuya.jpg)](/wiki/File:Early_summer_night_Shibuya.jpg)

The walking dinosaur advertisement visible on the QFRONT (Tsutaya) building seen in the 2003 film *[Lost in Translation](/wiki/Lost_in_Translation_(film) "Lost in Translation (film)")*[[17]](#cite_note-17)

Shibuya Crossing is often featured in films and television shows which take place in Tokyo, such as *[Lost in Translation](/wiki/Lost_in_Translation_(film) "Lost in Translation (film)")*,[[18]](#cite_note-18) *[The Fast and the Furious: Tokyo Drift](/wiki/The_Fast_and_the_Furious:_Tokyo_Drift "The Fast and the Furious: Tokyo Drift")*, *[Alice in Borderland](/wiki/Alice_in_Borderland_(TV_series) "Alice in Borderland (TV series)")*, *[Jujutsu Kaisen](/wiki/Jujutsu_Kaisen_season_2 "Jujutsu Kaisen season 2")*, *[Resident Evil: Retribution](/wiki/Resident_Evil:_Retribution "Resident Evil: Retribution")*, *[Sonic The Hedgehog 3](/wiki/Sonic_the_Hedgehog_3_(film) "Sonic the Hedgehog 3 (film)")*, as well as on domestic and international news broadcasts. The iconic video screen featured in the above films, in particular *Lost in Translation* with its 'walking dinosaur' scene, was taken down for a period of time and replaced with static advertising, although it resumed operation in July 2013.[[19]](#cite_note-19)

Shibuya Crossing has also been featured in many video games like *[Persona 5](/wiki/Persona_5 "Persona 5")*, *[The World Ends with You](/wiki/The_World_Ends_with_You "The World Ends with You")*, *[Chaos;Head](/wiki/Chaos;Head "Chaos;Head")* and *[Forza Horizon 6](/wiki/Forza_Horizon_6 "Forza Horizon 6")*.[[20]](#cite_note-20)[[21]](#cite_note-21)

[Welsh](/wiki/Wales "Wales") rock band [Manic Street Preachers](/wiki/Manic_Street_Preachers "Manic Street Preachers") featured the area prominently in the [music video](/wiki/Music_video "Music video") for the song "[Motorcycle Emptiness](/wiki/Motorcycle_Emptiness "Motorcycle Emptiness")" from the album [Generation Terrorists](/wiki/Generation_Terrorists "Generation Terrorists") in 1992. The single peaked at number 17 in the [UK singles chart](/wiki/UK_singles_chart "UK singles chart") and was later certified silver by the [British Phonographic Industry](/wiki/British_Phonographic_Industry "British Phonographic Industry") for sales of 200,000 copies.[[22]](#cite_note-22)[[23]](#cite_note-23)

Contemporary British painter [Carl Randall](/wiki/Carl_Randall "Carl Randall") (who spent 10 years living in Tokyo as an artist) depicted the area in his large artwork *Shibuya*, exhibited at the National Portrait Gallery in London 2013.[[24]](#cite_note-BBC_Carl_Randall_interview-24)[[25]](#cite_note-bp_travel_award_2012-25)[[26]](#cite_note-Shibuya-26)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Shibuya_Crossing&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: See also")]
